title: Today's AI Brief: Māori Text-to-Speech Model Spurns Big Tech’s Values
summary: A Māori text-to-speech model challenges big tech values, and what happens when companies go too AI-pilled.
imageAlt: Image from source article for Māori Text-to-Speech Model Spurns Big Tech’s Values
imageCaption: Māori Text-to-Speech Model Spurns Big Tech’s Values
imageSourceTitle: Māori Text-to-Speech Model Spurns Big Tech’s Values

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to today's AI Brief. I'm looking at some interesting developments in the world of artificial intelligence. New Zealand's linguistic landscape is getting a boost with a new Māori text-to-speech model that's making waves. It's great to see more languages being represented in the AI space, and I think this could be a game-changer for the Māori community.

First up, a Māori text-to-speech model is challenging big tech's values. This model is part of a larger effort to preserve and promote the Māori language, one of New Zealand's three official languages. What's fascinating here is that this model is being developed with a focus on cultural sensitivity and linguistic accuracy, rather than just being another voice assistant for the masses. I love seeing AI projects that prioritize cultural sensitivity – it's a great reminder that tech can be a force for good.

Next, I'm seeing what happens when companies become too AI-pilled. According to Box founder Aaron Levie, the people pushing AI are often the ones who don't understand what jobs AI is replacing. He calls this 'AI psychosis.' It's like they're drinking their own Kool-Aid – and it's getting a bit too real. I'm curious to see how this plays out, as AI continues to change the way we work.

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.
